chunk_summary: Guidelines for empiric antibiotic regimens in severe acute malnutrition (SAM), including step-down criteria for transitioning from IV/IM to oral therapy based on clinical improvement and renal safety considerations.
learning_objectives: Understand empiric antibiotic regimens for uncomplicated outpatient and complicated inpatient SAM cases.
Learn step-down criteria for transitioning from IV/IM to oral antibiotic therapy in SAM management.
Recognize renal safety protocols, including withholding gentamicin in cases of oliguria/anuria.
Identify escalation protocols for worsening clinical status or septic shock in SAM patients.
key_facts: For uncomplicated outpatient SAM, oral amoxicillin 15 mg/kg per dose every 8 hours for 5 days is recommended.
First-line regimen for complicated inpatient SAM: Ampicillin 50 mg/kg every 6 hours plus gentamicin 7.5 mg/kg once daily for 7 days.
Add cloxacillin 100 mg/kg/day in 4 divided doses for staphylococcal infections with extensive skin lesions or abscesses.
Escalate therapy at 48–72 hours if septic shock or worsening clinical status occurs; options include cefotaxime 150 mg/kg/day or ceftriaxone 100 mg/kg/day.
Withhold gentamicin if oliguria/anuria is present and reassess hydration, perfusion, and renal function before restarting.
Step down to oral therapy once the child is clinically improving, tolerating enteral feeds, and can complete the course reliably.
reference_quote: Empiric antibiotics in SAM must be 'ready to prescribe,' because invasive infection can present without fever and delays increase mortality. (MoHFW, 2011)
document_key_topics: Renal safety protocols in SAM management
Escalation guidelines for septic shock or worsening clinical status
